name: Suez Crisis
cause: Suez Canal closed ~5 months after the Anglo-French-Israeli invasion
disruption: canal fully blocked; ~1.5 Mb/d of oil flows rerouted
outcome: European fuel rationing; tanker rates roughly tripled; US supplied emergency crude
summary: Egypt blocked the canal with scuttled ships. Europe, fed mostly via Suez, faced petrol rationing within weeks while tankers detoured around the Cape. Prices rose modestly because US spare capacity filled the gap.

name: Six-Day War canal closure
cause: Suez Canal closed for 8 years after the Six-Day War
outcome: supertanker fleet built to make the Cape route economic; price impact small
summary: The canal shut for eight years. Oil kept flowing — the industry simply built bigger ships for the Cape route. A key lesson the model encodes: chokepoint loss becomes freight cost, not permanent shortage.

name: OPEC oil embargo
cause: Arab OPEC members embargoed the US/allies and cut output ~25%
outcome: oil quadrupled $3→$12; global recession; US GDP fell; strategic reserves invented
summary: The benchmark supply shock: a coordinated cut plus embargo quadrupled prices in months and tipped the West into recession. This is the ceiling case — a deliberate, sustained, coordinated cut with no spare capacity behind it.

name: Iranian Revolution
cause: Iranian production collapsed from ~6 to ~1.5 Mb/d
outcome: prices more than doubled through 1979-80; second global oil recession
summary: Revolution took most Iranian barrels off the market. Panic buying and hoarding amplified a ~5% world supply loss into a 150% price rise — the classic example of sentiment overshooting physical loss, which the model's overshoot factor encodes.

name: Iran–Iraq War outbreak
cause: war removed ~4 Mb/d of combined output
outcome: prices peaked in 1981 then eroded as other producers filled in
summary: Two major producers went to war and Gulf shipping came under threat, yet the price move was far smaller than 1979 — demand was already falling and Saudi spare capacity filled the gap. Mitigation capacity matters as much as the shock.

name: Tanker War
cause: Iran and Iraq attacked ~450 commercial vessels in the Gulf over years
disruption: Hormuz traffic harassed, never closed; war-risk insurance soared
outcome: prices FELL through the period (1980s glut); insurance and freight premiums rose sharply
summary: Hundreds of tanker attacks near Hormuz — and crude fell anyway, because the world was in a supply glut. The cost showed up in insurance and freight, not barrels. Proof that Hormuz risk prices through freight when flows continue.

name: Iraq invades Kuwait
cause: invasion + embargo removed ~4.3 Mb/d (Iraq + Kuwait)
outcome: Brent roughly doubled $17→$40 in two months; US recession followed
summary: A sudden 4+ Mb/d loss doubled prices in two months. The spike faded once Saudi ramp-up and IEA reserve releases arrived — the overshoot-then-mean-revert shape the engine reproduces.

name: Operation Desert Storm begins
cause: war began; simultaneous IEA strategic release announced
outcome: oil fell ~$10 (a third) in a single day — largest one-day drop then on record
summary: Prices collapsed the night the war started: the feared supply loss was already priced in, and coordinated reserve releases landed at once. War can be bearish when the risk premium built beforehand exceeds the reality.

name: OPEC coordinated cuts
cause: OPEC + allies cut ~2.1 Mb/d in stages after the Asian crisis glut
outcome: crude climbed from ~$10 to ~$25 within a year
summary: Proof that deliberate quota cuts move prices as much as wars when they persist: a ~2 Mb/d sustained cut more than doubled crude within a year from a very low base.

name: Venezuelan general strike
cause: PDVSA strike halted ~2.3 Mb/d for two months
outcome: crude rose ~30%; US drew reserves; output never fully recovered
summary: A two-month national strike removed over 2 Mb/d. Prices rose about 30% and settled once other producers back-filled — a mid-sized, time-limited supply loss the model's decay mode mirrors.

name: Iraq War begins
cause: invasion; Iraqi exports (~2 Mb/d) briefly offline
outcome: prices fell ~25% within weeks of the invasion as the war-risk premium unwound
summary: Like 1991: the premium was priced before the first strike, and crude fell once uncertainty resolved. Markets price probabilities, not events — the reason the model treats sentiment overshoot as front-loaded.

name: Hurricane Katrina
cause: ~1.5 Mb/d Gulf of Mexico production + major refining offline
outcome: crude rose ~$5; US petrol jumped ~45¢/gal; IEA released 60M bbl
summary: The refinery lesson: crude barely moved but pump prices jumped, because the loss was refining capacity, not oil. Matches the model's separate scarcity channel — pump pain without a world-price shock.

source.name: Wikipedia — Economic effects of Hurricane Katrina

name: Niger Delta militant attacks
cause: MEND attacks shut in 0.5–0.8 Mb/d for years
outcome: persistent ~$5–10 risk premium through the mid-2000s
summary: A chronic sub-1 Mb/d loss added a persistent risk premium rather than a spike — the low-severity, long-duration corner of the model's space.

source.name: Wikipedia — Conflict in the Niger Delta

name: Libyan civil war
cause: civil war halted ~1.6 Mb/d of light sweet crude
outcome: Brent rose ~$95→$125; IEA released 60M bbl to cap it
summary: A 1.6 Mb/d loss lifted Brent ~25% and held it there until reserves were released — a clean mid-scale calibration point between Abqaiq and the Gulf War.

name: Iran sanctions + Hormuz closure threats
cause: EU embargo cut Iranian exports ~1 Mb/d; Iran threatened to close Hormuz
outcome: Brent held above $110 with an estimated $10–15 Hormuz risk premium
summary: Hormuz was never touched, but the credible threat alone kept a $10–15 premium in every barrel for a year. Closure probability prices in long before any ship is stopped — exactly what the σ slider represents.

name: Fort McMurray wildfire
cause: wildfire shut ~1 Mb/d of oil-sands output
outcome: brief ~$2–3 lift; fully faded within a month
summary: A short, fully-recoverable 1 Mb/d outage barely registered — the market looked through it. Short disruptions with certain recovery price near zero, matching the model's shock mode.

name: US reimposes Iran sanctions
cause: sanctions expected to cut ~1.5 Mb/d; surprise waivers reversed the trade
outcome: Brent rose to $86, then crashed ~40% in Q4 when waivers landed
summary: Prices climbed all year on expected losses, then collapsed when waivers meant the barrels stayed. Both legs — anticipation and reversal — are sentiment moves around the same physical facts.

name: Abqaiq–Khurais drone attack
cause: drone strike on Saudi processing removed 5.7 Mb/d (~5% of world supply) overnight
outcome: Brent jumped ~15% in a day (biggest since 1991) and fully faded within weeks
summary: The cleanest modern datapoint: the largest instantaneous supply loss ever recorded moved crude just 15%, because recovery was fast and certain. Severity times expected duration — not severity alone — sets the price.

name: COVID demand collapse
cause: global lockdowns cut oil demand ~20%
outcome: Brent fell ~65%; WTI futures briefly went negative
summary: The inverse shock: demand vanished, prices collapsed, storage overflowed. Included as the contrast case — the same elasticity mathematics running in reverse.

source.name: Wikipedia — 2020 Russia–Saudi Arabia oil price war

name: OPEC+ 9.7 Mb/d cut
cause: largest coordinated cut in history to rescue the COVID crash
outcome: crude roughly doubled off the April 2020 low within two months
summary: The largest cut ever agreed lifted crude ~90% off its floor in two months — the upper bound of what deliberate supply management can do, from an extreme low base.

name: Ever Given Suez blockage
cause: container ship grounded, blocking the canal for 6 days
disruption: canal 100% blocked, 6 days; ~$9.6bn/day of trade queued
outcome: crude blipped ~4% and fully retraced; container freight disrupted for weeks
summary: Six days of total Suez closure moved crude only ~4%. Chokepoint blockages price by expected duration; the freight system, not the oil price, absorbed the hit.

name: Russian invasion of Ukraine
cause: self-sanctioning and embargo fears on ~2–3 Mb/d of Russian exports
disruption: feared multi-Mb/d loss; actual rerouting to Asia
outcome: Brent hit $139 intraday; India: pump +₹10/L before excise cuts froze it, INR fell ~10%, CAD widened
summary: The calibration episode. Crude spiked ~50% sustained; India's pump price rose ₹10 then was frozen by excise cuts while the rupee slid. The exact policy-damping behaviour the model's 0.5 pass-through encodes.

name: US SPR 180M bbl release
cause: largest strategic release ever: ~1 Mb/d added for 6 months
outcome: estimated to have shaved $10–15 off crude through 2022
summary: The counter-episode: strategic reserves work in reverse too. An added 1 Mb/d for six months took roughly a tenth off the price — the same elasticity, opposite sign.

name: OPEC+ surprise cut
cause: surprise ~1.16 Mb/d voluntary cut announced in April
outcome: crude jumped ~6% on announcement day
summary: A one-million-barrel surprise cut bought a single-digit percentage move in a well-supplied market — the modern, muted end of OPEC power.

name: Saudi 'lollipop' cut
cause: Saudi Arabia's extra voluntary 1 Mb/d cut from July
outcome: Brent climbed ~$75→$95 through Q3 2023
summary: A sustained unilateral 1 Mb/d cut lifted crude ~10–25% over a quarter — slower and steadier than a shock, the sustained-mode shape.

name: Red Sea crisis (Houthi attacks)
cause: attacks on shipping cut Suez/Bab-el-Mandeb transits by ~50%+
disruption: half of Red Sea traffic rerouted via the Cape (+10–14 days)
outcome: crude nearly flat; container freight rates doubled-to-tripled; insurance soared; Russian cargoes to India kept transiting
summary: The definitive Red Sea datapoint: transits halved, yet crude barely moved — the cost surfaced in freight and insurance, and rerouting absorbed the rest. Exactly the cost-led, low-shortfall behaviour the model's Red Sea channel produces.

name: Gulf of Oman tanker attacks & seizures
cause: limpet-mine attacks and tanker seizures near Hormuz
disruption: no flow loss; war-risk insurance up ~10x for Gulf transits
outcome: brief single-digit price moves; large insurance/freight premiums
summary: Attacks at the strait's mouth without closure: prices blipped single digits while insurance costs exploded. Low-σ Hormuz events are freight events, not crude events.

name: OPEC declines to cut (shale glut)
cause: OPEC kept pumping into a US-shale supply glut
outcome: crude fell ~60% over 18 months; producer economies strained
summary: The zero-disruption baseline: with no shock and abundant supply, prices collapsed. Included so near-zero scenarios retrieve a genuinely quiet analog.
